tu.prop: Plastics in the marine environment have become a major concern because of their persistence at sea, and adverse consequences to marine life and potentially human health.

tuv.seg: Abstract
Résumé

tu.prop: Implementing mitigation strategies requires an understanding and quantification of marine plastic sources, taking spatial and temporal variability into account.

tuv.seg: Plastics in the marine environment have become a major concern because of their persistence at sea, and adverse consequences to marine life and potentially human health.
Le plastique persiste dans le milieu marin, ce qui en fait une préoccupation majeure en raison des conséquences néfastes sur la vie marine et possiblement sur la santé humaine.

tu.prop: Plastics in the marine environment have become a major concern because of their persistence at sea, and adverse consequences to marine life and potentially human health.
Here we present a global model of plastic inputs from rivers into oceans based on waste management, population density and hydrological information.

tuv.seg: Implementing mitigation strategies requires an understanding and quantification of marine plastic sources, taking spatial and temporal variability into account.
La mise en place de stratégies d'atténuation demande une compréhension de l'origine du plastique marin et sa quantification, en prenant en compte la variabilité spatiale et temporelle.

tu.prop: Implementing mitigation strategies requires an understanding and quantification of marine plastic sources, taking spatial and temporal variability into account.
Our model is calibrated against measurements available in the literature.

tuv.seg: Here we present a global model of plastic inputs from rivers into oceans based on waste management, population density and hydrological information.

Ce travail présente un modèle global des apports de plastique des rivières vers les océans. La modélisation s'appuie sur des données sur la gestion des déchets, la densité de la population et les informations hydrologiques.

tu.prop: Here we present a global model of plastic inputs from rivers into oceans based on waste management, population density and hydrological information.
We estimate that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic waste currently enters the ocean every year from rivers, with over 74% of emissions occurring between May and October.

tuv.seg: Our model is calibrated against measurements available in the literature.
Notre modèle est calibré sur les mesures disponibles dans la littérature.

tu.prop: Our model is calibrated against measurements available in the literature.
The top 20 polluting rivers, mostly located in Asia, account for 67% of the global total.

tuv.seg: We estimate that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic waste currently enters the ocean every year from rivers, with over 74% of emissions occurring between May and October.

Nous estimons qu'actuellement, entre 1,15 et 2,41 millions de tonnes de déchets plastiques passent des rivières aux océans chaque année. La majorité des émissions (74 %) se produit entre mai et octobre.

tu.prop: We estimate that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic waste currently enters the ocean every year from rivers, with over 74% of emissions occurring between May and October.
The findings of this study provide baseline data for ocean plastic mass balance exercises, and assist in prioritizing future plastic debris monitoring and mitigation strategies.

tuv.seg: The top 20 polluting rivers, mostly located in Asia, account for 67% of the global total.
Les vingt rivières les plus polluantes, principalement situées en Asie, participent à hauteur de 67 % des émissions totales.

tu.prop: The top 20 polluting rivers, mostly located in Asia, account for 67% of the global total.
Similar content being viewed by others

tuv.seg: The findings of this study provide baseline data for ocean plastic mass balance exercises, and assist in prioritizing future plastic debris monitoring and mitigation strategies.

Les résultats de cette étude mettent à disposition des données de référence à utiliser pour des bilans de masse du plastique océanique et pour établir les priorités des stratégies futures de surveillance et d'atténuation des déchets plastiques.

tu.prop: The findings of this study provide baseline data for ocean plastic mass balance exercises, and assist in prioritizing future plastic debris monitoring and mitigation strategies.

tu.prop: Plastics are increasingly used worldwide in a wide variety of applications with global production exceeding 300 million tonnes per year since 2014 (ref.

tuv.seg: Introduction
Introduction
Plastics are increasingly used worldwide in a wide variety of applications with global production exceeding 300 million tonnes per year since 2014 (ref.
Les plastiques sont de plus en plus utilisés dans le monde entier, à des fins très variées. Leur production mondiale dépasse les 300 millions de tonnes par an depuis 2014 (ref. 1).

tu.prop: Plastics are increasingly used worldwide in a wide variety of applications with global production exceeding 300 million tonnes per year since 2014 (ref.
Because of their durability, low-recycling rates, poor waste management and maritime use, a significant portion of the plastics produced worldwide enters and persists in marine ecosystems2.
This includes shoreline, seabed, water column and sea surface environments of the world’s oceans3.

tuv.seg: Because of their durability, low-recycling rates, poor waste management and maritime use, a significant portion of the plastics produced worldwide enters and persists in marine ecosystems2.

Sa durée de vie, son faible taux de recyclage, son utilisation en mer et la mauvaise gestion des déchets plastique expliquent qu'une proportion importante du plastique produit dans le monde entre dans les écosystèmes marins et y reste.

tu.prop: Because of their durability, low-recycling rates, poor waste management and maritime use, a significant portion of the plastics produced worldwide enters and persists in marine ecosystems2.

The release of plastics into the marine environment occurs through a variety of pathways, including river and atmospheric transport, beach littering and directly at sea via aquaculture, shipping and fishing activities4.

tuv.seg: This includes shoreline, seabed, water column and sea surface environments of the world’s oceans3.
Cela inclut les littoraux, les fonds marins, les colonnes d'eau et les eaux de surface de l'océan mondial[3].

tu.prop: This includes shoreline, seabed, water column and sea surface environments of the world’s oceans3.
A comprehensive risk assessment of this relatively new type of marine contamination requires defining, understanding and quantifying emissions both geographically and temporally.

tuv.seg: The release of plastics into the marine environment occurs through a variety of pathways, including river and atmospheric transport, beach littering and directly at sea via aquaculture, shipping and fishing activities4.

Le plastique est rejeté dans l'environnement marin via de nombreux chemins : il peut être transporté par les rivières et par les airs, venir de plages polluées, et directement en mer via l'aquaculture, les bateaux et les activités de pêche[4].

tu.prop: The release of plastics into the marine environment occurs through a variety of pathways, including river and atmospheric transport, beach littering and directly at sea via aquaculture, shipping and fishing activities4.
This knowledge helps in refining our understanding of marine plastic pollution sources and pathways, while working towards an estimated global budget for ocean plastics.

tuv.seg: A comprehensive risk assessment of this relatively new type of marine contamination requires defining, understanding and quantifying emissions both geographically and temporally.

Une évaluation complète des risques liés à ce type relativement nouveau de contamination marine nécessite de définir, comprendre et quantifier les émissions à la fois géographiquement et temporellement.

tu.prop: A comprehensive risk assessment of this relatively new type of marine contamination requires defining, understanding and quantifying emissions both geographically and temporally.
It also assists in identifying the critical locations and seasons of plastic releases, supporting the implementation of cost-effective monitoring and source mitigation efforts.

tuv.seg: This knowledge helps in refining our understanding of marine plastic pollution sources and pathways, while working towards an estimated global budget for ocean plastics.

Acquérir ces connaissances nous permettra d'affiner notre compréhension des sources de la pollution plastique et des routes qu'elle emprunte, et nous permettra aussi d'estimer le coût global du plastique dans les océans.

tu.prop: This knowledge helps in refining our understanding of marine plastic pollution sources and pathways, while working towards an estimated global budget for ocean plastics.
Land-based sources, as opposed to marine-based sources, are considered the dominant input of plastics into oceans4.

tuv.seg: It also assists in identifying the critical locations and seasons of plastic releases, supporting the implementation of cost-effective monitoring and source mitigation efforts.

Le but est aussi d'identifier les zones et les saisons critiques en termes d'émissions plastiques, pour servir à mettre en place un suivi économiquement viable et encourager une réduction à la source.

tu.prop: It also assists in identifying the critical locations and seasons of plastic releases, supporting the implementation of cost-effective monitoring and source mitigation efforts.

While a quantification of land-based inputs from coastal populations (<50 km from coastline) worldwide already exists5, no global assessment of contributions from inland populations through riverine systems has been proposed to date.

tuv.seg: Land-based sources, as opposed to marine-based sources, are considered the dominant input of plastics into oceans4.
Les sources terrestres, à la différence des sources marines, seraient le principal apport de plastique dans les océans[4].

tu.prop: Land-based sources, as opposed to marine-based sources, are considered the dominant input of plastics into oceans4.
Only a few local studies have reported levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide6.

tuv.seg: While a quantification of land-based inputs from coastal populations (<50 km from coastline) worldwide already exists5, no global assessment of contributions from inland populations through riverine systems has been proposed to date.

Jusqu'à maintenant, aucune évaluation mondiale, concernant les contributions des populations éloignées du littoral à partir des systèmes fluviaux n’a été proposée, alors qu'il existe déjà[5] une quantification des apports terrestres par les populations côtières (moins de 50 km du littoral) du monde.

tu.prop: While a quantification of land-based inputs from coastal populations (<50 km from coastline) worldwide already exists5, no global assessment of contributions from inland populations through riverine systems has been proposed to date.
Such freshwater studies generally focus on micro-plastics (< 5 mm length) contaminating sediment and waters of lakes and rivers.

tuv.seg: Only a few local studies have reported levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide6.
Seules quelques études locales ont fait état des niveaux de pollution plastique dans les systèmes d’eau douce du monde entier[6].

tu.prop: Only a few local studies have reported levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide6.
Sampling design and devices, as well as reported units (for example, pieces per m2, grams per m3) vary substantially between different assessments7.

tuv.seg: Such freshwater studies generally focus on micro-plastics (< 5 mm length) contaminating sediment and waters of lakes and rivers.
En règle générale, les études en eau douce se concentrent sur les micro-plastiques (moins de 5 mn d'épaisseur) qui contaminent les sédiments ainsi que l’eau des lacs et des rivières.

tu.prop: Such freshwater studies generally focus on micro-plastics (< 5 mm length) contaminating sediment and waters of lakes and rivers.

Overall, observed plastic concentrations differ by several orders of magnitude in between sampled rivers7, with studies suggesting that population density8,9, levels of urbanization and industrialization within catchment areas10,11, rainfall rates10,12 and the presence of artificial barriers such as weirs and dams13 play a significant role in resulting rates of river-based plastic inputs into the ocean.

tuv.seg: Sampling design and devices, as well as reported units (for example, pieces per m2, grams per m3) vary substantially between different assessments7.
La conception et les dispositifs d’échantillonnage, ainsi que les unités rapportées (par exemple particules par m², grammes par m³) varient considérablement entre les différentes études[7].

tu.prop: Sampling design and devices, as well as reported units (for example, pieces per m2, grams per m3) vary substantially between different assessments7.
A study in California12 was the first to report levels of micro-plastics in river surface waters, with sampling in Los Angeles River, San Gabriel River and tributary Coyote Creek.

tuv.seg: Overall, observed plastic concentrations differ by several orders of magnitude in between sampled rivers7, with studies suggesting that population density8,9, levels of urbanization and industrialization within catchment areas10,11, rainfall rates10,12 and the presence of artificial barriers such as weirs and dams13 play a significant role in resulting rates of river-based plastic inputs into the ocean.

Globalement, les concentrations de plastique observées diffèrent de plusieurs ordres de grandeur entre les rivières échantillonnées[7], les études suggérant que la densité de population[8],[9], le niveau d’urbanisation et d’industrialisation au sein de bassins versants[10],[11], les taux de précipitations[10],[12] et la présence de barrières artificielles telles que les barrages et les déversoirs[13], jouent un rôle déterminant dans les résultats des taux de plastique provenant des rivières et se retrouvant dans l’océan.

tu.prop: Overall, observed plastic concentrations differ by several orders of magnitude in between sampled rivers7, with studies suggesting that population density8,9, levels of urbanization and industrialization within catchment areas10,11, rainfall rates10,12 and the presence of artificial barriers such as weirs and dams13 play a significant role in resulting rates of river-based plastic inputs into the ocean.
The report found substantial temporal variations in plastic contamination levels.

tuv.seg: A study in California12 was the first to report levels of micro-plastics in river surface waters, with sampling in Los Angeles River, San Gabriel River and tributary Coyote Creek.

Une étude menée en Californie[12] est la première à avoir relevé les niveaux de microplastiques dans les eaux de surface de fleuves grâce à ses échantillons provenant des fleuves Los Angeles et San Gabriel, et de l’affluent Coyote Creek.

tu.prop: A study in California12 was the first to report levels of micro-plastics in river surface waters, with sampling in Los Angeles River, San Gabriel River and tributary Coyote Creek.
For a given location, the study found up to three orders of magnitude differences between plastic concentrations measured at different time periods.

tuv.seg: The report found substantial temporal variations in plastic contamination levels.
Le rapport fait état de variations temporelles considérables des niveaux de pollution plastique.

tu.prop: The report found substantial temporal variations in plastic contamination levels.
These variations were mostly explained by events of dry and wet weather, implying that runoff plays an important role in the transport of plastics into freshwater systems.

tuv.seg: For a given location, the study found up to three orders of magnitude differences between plastic concentrations measured at different time periods.

Pour un emplacement donné, cette étude a constaté des différences pouvant aller jusqu’à trois ordres de grandeur entre les concentrations de plastiques selon les périodes auxquelles les mesures ont été prises.

tu.prop: For a given location, the study found up to three orders of magnitude differences between plastic concentrations measured at different time periods.
In recent years, more studies sampled plastic in surface waters of rivers.

tuv.seg: These variations were mostly explained by events of dry and wet weather, implying that runoff plays an important role in the transport of plastics into freshwater systems.

Ces variations s’expliquent principalement par les cas de temps secs et humides, cela implique que les ruissellements ont un rôle important dans le transport des matières plastiques dans les systèmes d’eau douce.

tu.prop: These variations were mostly explained by events of dry and wet weather, implying that runoff plays an important role in the transport of plastics into freshwater systems.
In Europe, studies estimated that the Danube River releases 530–1,500 tonnes of plastic into the Black Sea annually8,9.

tuv.seg: In recent years, more studies sampled plastic in surface waters of rivers.
Ces dernières années, d’autres études ont prélevé des échantillons de plastiques provenant des eaux de surface des rivières.

tu.prop: In recent years, more studies sampled plastic in surface waters of rivers.

Another European study estimated that 20–31 tonnes flows into the North Sea every year from the Rhine River8, with different locations along this river demonstrating the presence of significant sources (for example, wastewater treatment plants, tributaries) and sinks (for example, weirs; ref.

tuv.seg: In Europe, studies estimated that the Danube River releases 530–1,500 tonnes of plastic into the Black Sea annually8,9.
En Europe, des études ont estimé que le Danube rejette 530 à 1 500 tonnes de plastique par an dans la Mer Noire[8],[9].

tu.prop: In Europe, studies estimated that the Danube River releases 530–1,500 tonnes of plastic into the Black Sea annually8,9.

tuv.seg: Another European study estimated that 20–31 tonnes flows into the North Sea every year from the Rhine River8, with different locations along this river demonstrating the presence of significant sources (for example, wastewater treatment plants, tributaries) and sinks (for example, weirs; ref.

Une autre étude européenne a estimé que 20 à 31 tonnes de plastique se déversent chaque année dans la Mer du Nord à partir du Rhin[8]. Plusieurs emplacements le long du fleuve démontrent la présence de puits (par exemple, déversoirs ref.13) et de sources (stations d’épuration, affluents, par exemple) importantes.

tu.prop: Another European study estimated that 20–31 tonnes flows into the North Sea every year from the Rhine River8, with different locations along this river demonstrating the presence of significant sources (for example, wastewater treatment plants, tributaries) and sinks (for example, weirs; ref.
In the Italian Po River, sampled concentrations differed by one order of magnitude between winter and spring14, emphasising seasonality of freshwater contamination in rivers.
Surface plastic concentrations showed a statistically significant correlation with human population densities and proportion of urban development in catchments around the Chesapeake Bay (USA)10.

tuv.seg: In the Italian Po River, sampled concentrations differed by one order of magnitude between winter and spring14, emphasising seasonality of freshwater contamination in rivers.

Dans le fleuve italien Pô, les concentrations prélevées diffèrent d'un ordre de grandeur entre l'hiver et le printemps[14]. Cela démontre l'influence saisonnière de la pollution des eaux douces dans les rivières.

tu.prop: In the Italian Po River, sampled concentrations differed by one order of magnitude between winter and spring14, emphasising seasonality of freshwater contamination in rivers.

The study also reported the highest measured micro-plastic concentrations (up to 1.6 milligrams per cubic metre for 1.73 particles per cubic metre) occurring after major rain events at three of the four monitoring sites.

tuv.seg: Surface plastic concentrations showed a statistically significant correlation with human population densities and proportion of urban development in catchments around the Chesapeake Bay (USA)10.

On a observé une corrélation statistiquement significative entre les concentrations de plastique à la surface, les densités de population et le degré de développement urbain dans les bassins versants autour de la baie de Chesapeake (États-Unis)[10].

tu.prop: Surface plastic concentrations showed a statistically significant correlation with human population densities and proportion of urban development in catchments around the Chesapeake Bay (USA)10.

In South America, monitoring from scientists and volunteers at the Elqui, Maipo, Biobio and Maule Rivers, demonstrated a consistent pattern between numerical concentration of micro-plastics (range 0.05–0.74 particles per cubic metre) and the presence of litter deposition on riversides15.

tuv.seg: The study also reported the highest measured micro-plastic concentrations (up to 1.6 milligrams per cubic metre for 1.73 particles per cubic metre) occurring after major rain events at three of the four monitoring sites.

L'étude montre également que, sur trois des quatre sites de surveillance, le plus haut taux de concentration de microplastiques (jusqu'à 1,6 milligramme par mètre cube pour 1,73 particule par mètre cube) survenait après des épisodes de pluie intense.

tu.prop: The study also reported the highest measured micro-plastic concentrations (up to 1.6 milligrams per cubic metre for 1.73 particles per cubic metre) occurring after major rain events at three of the four monitoring sites.
In Asia, sampling of micro-plastics on beaches suggested that the nearby Pearl River is a major source of plastic pollution for the region16.

tuv.seg: In South America, monitoring from scientists and volunteers at the Elqui, Maipo, Biobio and Maule Rivers, demonstrated a consistent pattern between numerical concentration of micro-plastics (range 0.05–0.74 particles per cubic metre) and the presence of litter deposition on riversides15.

En Amérique du Sud, des scientifiques et bénévoles surveillent les fleuves Elqui, Maipo, Biobio et Maule, ce qui a permis d'observer une constante entre la concentration numérique de microplastiques (de 0,05 à 0,74 particule par mètre cube) et la présence de déchets sur les berges[15].

tu.prop: In South America, monitoring from scientists and volunteers at the Elqui, Maipo, Biobio and Maule Rivers, demonstrated a consistent pattern between numerical concentration of micro-plastics (range 0.05–0.74 particles per cubic metre) and the presence of litter deposition on riversides15.
Surface samplings at the Chinese Yangtze River mouth showed considerably higher plastic concentrations than any other sampled river worldwide17 with a reported 4,137 particles per cubic metre.

tuv.seg: In Asia, sampling of micro-plastics on beaches suggested that the nearby Pearl River is a major source of plastic pollution for the region16.
En Asie, la rivière des Perles est une source majeure de pollution plastique dans la région, comme le laissent penser les échantillons de microplastiques collectés sur les plages voisines[16].

tu.prop: In Asia, sampling of micro-plastics on beaches suggested that the nearby Pearl River is a major source of plastic pollution for the region16.

The significant differences between sampled estuarine concentrations and nearshore monitoring in the area confirmed that the Yangtze River is a major regional source of plastic input into the marine environment.

tuv.seg: Surface samplings at the Chinese Yangtze River mouth showed considerably higher plastic concentrations than any other sampled river worldwide17 with a reported 4,137 particles per cubic metre.

Les échantillons prélevés à la surface de l'embouchure du Yangtze en Chine ont révélé des concentrations de plastique considérablement plus élevées que dans n'importe quel autre fleuve étudié dans le monde[17], avec 4 137 particules par mètre cube.

tu.prop: Surface samplings at the Chinese Yangtze River mouth showed considerably higher plastic concentrations than any other sampled river worldwide17 with a reported 4,137 particles per cubic metre.
Here we provide a global estimate of river plastic inputs into the world’s oceans, considering both the seasonality and spatial variability of local sources.

tuv.seg: The significant differences between sampled estuarine concentrations and nearshore monitoring in the area confirmed that the Yangtze River is a major regional source of plastic input into the marine environment.

L'écart entre la concentration dans les échantillons prélevés à l'estuaire et celle dans les zones de surveillance littorales prouve que le fleuve Yangtze est une des sources régionales principales d'apport de plastique dans les environnements marins.

tu.prop: The significant differences between sampled estuarine concentrations and nearshore monitoring in the area confirmed that the Yangtze River is a major regional source of plastic input into the marine environment.

Our global model uses geospatial data of population density18,19, rates of mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per inhabitant and per country5,20, monthly catchment runoff21,22, as well as the presence of artificial barriers (for example, dams and weirs) that act as particle sinks23,24.

tuv.seg: Here we provide a global estimate of river plastic inputs into the world’s oceans, considering both the seasonality and spatial variability of local sources.

Nous fournissons ici une estimation à échelle mondiale des apports fluviaux de plastique dans les océans, nous prenons à la fois en compte les saisonnalités et de la variabilité spatiale des sources locales.

tu.prop: Here we provide a global estimate of river plastic inputs into the world’s oceans, considering both the seasonality and spatial variability of local sources.

The correlation found between model outputs and field observations suggests that the model presented here is able to describe a significant proportion of the spatio-temporal variations on levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide.

tuv.seg: Our global model uses geospatial data of population density18,19, rates of mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per inhabitant and per country5,20, monthly catchment runoff21,22, as well as the presence of artificial barriers (for example, dams and weirs) that act as particle sinks23,24.

Nous basons notre modèle global sur des données géospatiales de densité de population[18],[19], taux de production de déchets plastiques mal gérés (MPW) par pays et par habitant [5],[20], ruissellement mensuel des bassins versants [21],[22] ainsi que sur l'existence de barrages artificiels (barrages et déversoirs par exemple) agissant comme des puits à particules[23],[24].

tu.prop: Our global model uses geospatial data of population density18,19, rates of mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per inhabitant and per country5,20, monthly catchment runoff21,22, as well as the presence of artificial barriers (for example, dams and weirs) that act as particle sinks23,24.
We predict a global annual input of plastic from rivers into the oceans ranging from 1.15 to 2.41 million tonnes with a dominant contribution from rivers of the Asian continent.

tuv.seg: The correlation found between model outputs and field observations suggests that the model presented here is able to describe a significant proportion of the spatio-temporal variations on levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide.

La corrélation entre les résultats du modèle et ceux obtenus sur le terrain suggère l'efficacité de ce modèle à restituer les variations spatio-temporelles du niveau de pollution plastique dans les systèmes fluviaux dans le monde.

tu.prop: The correlation found between model outputs and field observations suggests that the model presented here is able to describe a significant proportion of the spatio-temporal variations on levels of plastic contamination in freshwater systems worldwide.

tuv.seg: We predict a global annual input of plastic from rivers into the oceans ranging from 1.15 to 2.41 million tonnes with a dominant contribution from rivers of the Asian continent.

Nous prévoyons une pollution plastique globale annuelle des océans comprise entre 1,15 et 2,41 millions de tonnes de déchets plastique provenant des rivières avec une contribution dominante des rivières asiatiques.

tu.prop: We predict a global annual input of plastic from rivers into the oceans ranging from 1.15 to 2.41 million tonnes with a dominant contribution from rivers of the Asian continent.
River plastic mass inputs to oceans

tuv.seg: Results
Résultats

tu.prop: We estimated that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic currently flows from the global riverine system into the oceans every year (Fig. 1).

tuv.seg: River plastic mass inputs to oceans
Émissions de plastique dans les océans du monde depuis les réseaux fluviaux

tu.prop: River plastic mass inputs to oceans

The top 20 polluting rivers were mostly located in Asia (Table 1) and accounted for more than two thirds (67%) of the global annual input while covering 2.2% of the continental surface area and representing 21% of the global population.

tuv.seg: We estimated that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic currently flows from the global riverine system into the oceans every year (Fig. 1).
Nous estimons qu'actuellement, entre 1,15 et 2,41 millions de tonnes de déchets plastiques passent des rivières aux océans chaque année (Fig.⁠1).

tu.prop: We estimated that between 1.15 and 2.41 million tonnes of plastic currently flows from the global riverine system into the oceans every year (Fig. 1).

Furthermore, the top 122 polluting rivers (4% of total landmass surface area and 36% of global population) contributed for >90% of the plastic inputs with 103 rivers located in Asia, eight in Africa, eight in South and Central America, and one in Europe.

tuv.seg: The top 20 polluting rivers were mostly located in Asia (Table 1) and accounted for more than two thirds (67%) of the global annual input while covering 2.2% of the continental surface area and representing 21% of the global population.

Les vingt rivières les plus polluantes, principalement situées en Asie (Tableau 1), participent à hauteur de 67 % des émissions totales alors qu'elles couvrent 2,2 %⁠des surfaces continentales et représentent 21 % de la population mondiale.

tu.prop: The top 20 polluting rivers were mostly located in Asia (Table 1) and accounted for more than two thirds (67%) of the global annual input while covering 2.2% of the continental surface area and representing 21% of the global population.
Figure 1: Mass of river plastic flowing into oceans in tonnes per year.

tuv.seg: Furthermore, the top 122 polluting rivers (4% of total landmass surface area and 36% of global population) contributed for >90% of the plastic inputs with 103 rivers located in Asia, eight in Africa, eight in South and Central America, and one in Europe.

En outre, les 122 cours d'eau les plus polluants (4 % de la surface totale de la masse continentale et 36 % de la population mondiale) contribuent à plus de 90 % des apports de plastique. 103 de ces fleuves sont situés en Asie, 8 en Afrique, 8 en Amérique du Sud et en Amérique centrale, et 1 en Europe.

tu.prop: Furthermore, the top 122 polluting rivers (4% of total landmass surface area and 36% of global population) contributed for >90% of the plastic inputs with 103 rivers located in Asia, eight in Africa, eight in South and Central America, and one in Europe.

Figure 1 : Masse de plastique fluvial se déversant dans les océans, en tonnes par an.

tu.prop: Figure 1: Mass of river plastic flowing into oceans in tonnes per year.

River contributions are derived from individual watershed characteristics such as population density (in inhab km−2), mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per country (in kg inhab−1 d−1) and monthly averaged runoff (in mm d−1).

tu.prop: The model is calibrated against river plastic concentration measurements from Europe, Asia, North and South America.

tuv.seg: River contributions are derived from individual watershed characteristics such as population density (in inhab km−2), mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per country (in kg inhab−1 d−1) and monthly averaged runoff (in mm d−1).

Les contributions des rivières sont dérivées des caractéristiques individuelles des bassins versants telles que la densité de population (en hab. par km−2), la production de déchets plastiques mal gérés (MPW) par pays (en kg par hab−1 d−1) et le ruissellement mensuel moyen (en mm par j−1). .

tu.prop: River contributions are derived from individual watershed characteristics such as population density (in inhab km−2), mismanaged plastic waste (MPW) production per country (in kg inhab−1 d−1) and monthly averaged runoff (in mm d−1).

tuv.seg: The model is calibrated against river plastic concentration measurements from Europe, Asia, North and South America.
Le modèle est calibré sur la base de mesures de concentration de plastique dans les rivières d'Europe, d'Asie, d'Amérique du Nord et du Sud.

tu.prop: The model is calibrated against river plastic concentration measurements from Europe, Asia, North and South America.

Our model was calibrated against reported micro- and macro-plastic concentrations from river surface waters of Europe, Asia, North and South America (Table 2).

tuv.seg: Full size table
Tableau en grand format

tu.prop: The studies used for this assessment report only a fraction of the wide plastic debris size spectrum: from particles larger than their net mesh sizes (typically 0.3 mm) to objects smaller than the aperture size of their sampling devices (typically 0.5 m).

tuv.seg: Our model was calibrated against reported micro- and macro-plastic concentrations from river surface waters of Europe, Asia, North and South America (Table 2).
Le modèle est calibré sur la base de mesures de concentration de plastique dans les rivières d'Europe, d'Asie, d'Amérique du Nord et du Sud.

tu.prop: Our model was calibrated against reported micro- and macro-plastic concentrations from river surface waters of Europe, Asia, North and South America (Table 2).
Therefore, our global river plastic input estimate is conservative, as we neglect the contribution of debris outside the sampled size range and below the surface sampling devices.

tuv.seg: The studies used for this assessment report only a fraction of the wide plastic debris size spectrum: from particles larger than their net mesh sizes (typically 0.3 mm) to objects smaller than the aperture size of their sampling devices (typically 0.5 m).

Les études utilisées pour cette évaluation ne couvrent qu'une fraction du large spectre de taille des débris plastiques : des particules plus grandes que la taille des mailles des filets (généralement 0,3 mm) aux objets plus petits que la taille de l'ouverture des dispositifs d'échantillonnage (généralement 0,5 m).

tu.prop: The studies used for this assessment report only a fraction of the wide plastic debris size spectrum: from particles larger than their net mesh sizes (typically 0.3 mm) to objects smaller than the aperture size of their sampling devices (typically 0.5 m).
Both buoyant and non-buoyant river plastics can be suspended throughout the water column and transported towards the sea due to turbulent river flows and large flood events25.

tuv.seg: Therefore, our global river plastic input estimate is conservative, as we neglect the contribution of debris outside the sampled size range and below the surface sampling devices.

Par conséquent, notre estimation de l'apport de plastique dans les fleuves est prudente, car nous négligeons la contribution des débris en dehors de la gamme de taille échantillonnée et en dessous des dispositifs d'échantillonnage d'eaux de surface.

tu.prop: Therefore, our global river plastic input estimate is conservative, as we neglect the contribution of debris outside the sampled size range and below the surface sampling devices.
Table 2 List of observational studies used in the calibration of the global river plastic inputs model.

tuv.seg: Both buoyant and non-buoyant river plastics can be suspended throughout the water column and transported towards the sea due to turbulent river flows and large flood events25.

Les plastiques flottants et non flottants issus des fleuves peuvent être accrochés à toute la colonne d'eau et transportés vers la mer en raison des débits de cours d'eau turbulents et de grandes crues [25].

tu.prop: Both buoyant and non-buoyant river plastics can be suspended throughout the water column and transported towards the sea due to turbulent river flows and large flood events25.

tu.prop: Table 2 List of observational studies used in the calibration of the global river plastic inputs model.

River plastic mass concentrations from the literature (Table 3) demonstrated a statistically significant, positive correlation with the product of annual MPW production rates inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff (Pearson product-moment test, r=0.4132, P<0.05, n=29).

tuv.seg: Full size table
Tableau en grand format

tu.prop: The correlation test was conducted after removing measurement data from the Yangtze River, considered as an outlier.

tuv.seg: River plastic mass concentrations from the literature (Table 3) demonstrated a statistically significant, positive correlation with the product of annual MPW production rates inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff (Pearson product-moment test, r=0.4132, P<0.05, n=29).

Pour formuler un modèle empirique, nous avons utilisé les données de la production annuelle de MPW à l'intérieur d'un bassin versant en aval des barrières artificielles et celles du ruissellement mensuel moyen du bassin versant pour le mois correspondant à la période d'échantillonnage.

tu.prop: River plastic mass concentrations from the literature (Table 3) demonstrated a statistically significant, positive correlation with the product of annual MPW production rates inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff (Pearson product-moment test, r=0.4132, P<0.05, n=29).
When including data from the Yangtze River, we logically obtained a much higher, yet biased correlation (r=0.99, P<0.05, n=30).

tuv.seg: The correlation test was conducted after removing measurement data from the Yangtze River, considered as an outlier.
Le test de corrélation a été effectué après avoir supprimé les données de mesure du fleuve Yangtsé, considérées comme aberrantes.

tu.prop: The correlation test was conducted after removing measurement data from the Yangtze River, considered as an outlier.
Table 3 Plastic concentration in sampled rivers and watershed characteristics.

tuv.seg: When including data from the Yangtze River, we logically obtained a much higher, yet biased correlation (r=0.99, P<0.05, n=30).
En incluant les données du fleuve Yangtsé, nous avons logiquement obtenu une corrélation beaucoup plus élevée, mais biaisée (r = 0,99; P > 0,05; n = 30).

tu.prop: When including data from the Yangtze River, we logically obtained a much higher, yet biased correlation (r=0.99, P<0.05, n=30).

tuv.seg: Table 3 Plastic concentration in sampled rivers and watershed characteristics.

The product of annual MPW production inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff for the month corresponding to the sampling period was used to formulate an empirical model.

tuv.seg: Full size table
Tableau en grand format

tu.prop: Uncertainties related to estimating daily flux inputs from concentration measurements were considered, while assessing a linear regression.

tuv.seg: The product of annual MPW production inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff for the month corresponding to the sampling period was used to formulate an empirical model.

Pour formuler un modèle empirique, nous avons utilisé les données de la production annuelle de MPW à l'intérieur d'un bassin versant en aval des barrières artificielles et celles du ruissellement mensuel moyen du bassin versant pour le mois correspondant à la période d'échantillonnage.

tu.prop: The product of annual MPW production inside a catchment area downstream of artificial barriers and monthly averaged catchment runoff for the month corresponding to the sampling period was used to formulate an empirical model.

We determined three parametric equations corresponding to lower, midpoint and upper model estimates to best predict estimation ranges from observations and account for these uncertainties (Supplementary Table 1).

tuv.seg: Uncertainties related to estimating daily flux inputs from concentration measurements were considered, while assessing a linear regression.
Les incertitudes liées à l'estimation des flux journaliers entrants à partir des mesures de concentration ont été prises en compte, lors de l'évaluation d'une régression linéaire.

tu.prop: Uncertainties related to estimating daily flux inputs from concentration measurements were considered, while assessing a linear regression.
Our midpoint calculations demonstrated a good relationship with mass flux inputs derived from measurements by orders of magnitude with a coefficient of determination r2=0.93 (n=30; Fig. 2).

tuv.seg: We determined three parametric equations corresponding to lower, midpoint and upper model estimates to best predict estimation ranges from observations and account for these uncertainties (Supplementary Table 1).

Nous avons déterminé trois équations paramétriques correspondant à des estimations inférieures, médianes et supérieures du modèle afin de prévoir au mieux les fourchettes d'estimation à partir des observations et de tenir compte des incertitudes déjà mentionnées (tableau complémentaire 1).

tu.prop: We determined three parametric equations corresponding to lower, midpoint and upper model estimates to best predict estimation ranges from observations and account for these uncertainties (Supplementary Table 1).
Figure 2: Comparison between river input model and measurements.

tuv.seg: Our midpoint calculations demonstrated a good relationship with mass flux inputs derived from measurements by orders of magnitude with a coefficient of determination r2=0.93 (n=30; Fig. 2).
Les estimations moyennes sont en accord avec la masse des entrées de flux mesurée par ordre de grandeur avec le coefficient de détermination r2 = 0,93 (n = 30 ; Fig.2).

tu.prop: Our midpoint calculations demonstrated a good relationship with mass flux inputs derived from measurements by orders of magnitude with a coefficient of determination r2=0.93 (n=30; Fig. 2).

Estimated plastic releases from Asian rivers represented 86% of the total global input.

tuv.seg: Full size image
Image en grand format

tu.prop: A considerably high-population density combined with relatively large MPW production rates and episodes of heavy rainfalls, resulted in this dominant contribution from the Asian continent, with an estimated annual input of 1.21 (range 1.00–2.06) million tonnes per year.

tuv.seg: Estimated plastic releases from Asian rivers represented 86% of the total global input.
Le taux de plastique provenant des rivières asiatiques est estimé à 86 % de l'apport mondial total.

tu.prop: Estimated plastic releases from Asian rivers represented 86% of the total global input.

Our model predicted that the Chinese Yangtze River is the largest contributing catchment, with an annual input of 0.33 (range 0.31–0.48) million tonnes of plastic discharged into the East China Sea, followed by the Ganges River catchment, between India and Bangladesh, with a computed input of 0.12 (range 0.10–0.17) million tonnes per year.

tuv.seg: A considerably high-population density combined with relatively large MPW production rates and episodes of heavy rainfalls, resulted in this dominant contribution from the Asian continent, with an estimated annual input of 1.21 (range 1.00–2.06) million tonnes per year.

Avec un apport annuel estimé à 1,21 million de tonnes (dans une fourchette de 1,00 à 2,06 millions), la contribution du continent asiatique domine du fait de sa densité de population nettement élevée combinée à des taux de production de MPW relativement importants et à des épisodes de fortes pluies.

tu.prop: A considerably high-population density combined with relatively large MPW production rates and episodes of heavy rainfalls, resulted in this dominant contribution from the Asian continent, with an estimated annual input of 1.21 (range 1.00–2.06) million tonnes per year.

The combined input of the Xi, Dong and Zhujiang Rivers in China all flowing into the South China Sea at the Pearl River delta, was estimated at 0.106 (range 0.091–0.169) million tonnes per year, placing the greater catchment into third position.

tuv.seg: Our model predicted that the Chinese Yangtze River is the largest contributing catchment, with an annual input of 0.33 (range 0.31–0.48) million tonnes of plastic discharged into the East China Sea, followed by the Ganges River catchment, between India and Bangladesh, with a computed input of 0.12 (range 0.10–0.17) million tonnes per year.

Selon les résultats fournis par notre modèle, le fleuve chinois Yangtze est le bassin versant qui contribue le plus à la pollution plastique, avec un apport annuel de 0,33 million de tonnes de plastique (dans une fourchette de 0,31 à 0,48 million) déversé dans la mer de Chine orientale, suivi par le bassin versant du Gange, entre l'Inde et le Bangladesh, avec un apport estimé à 0,12 million de tonnes par an (dans une fourchette de 0,10 à 0,17 million).

tu.prop: Our model predicted that the Chinese Yangtze River is the largest contributing catchment, with an annual input of 0.33 (range 0.31–0.48) million tonnes of plastic discharged into the East China Sea, followed by the Ganges River catchment, between India and Bangladesh, with a computed input of 0.12 (range 0.10–0.17) million tonnes per year.
Indonesia was also identified as a major contributor on the Asian continent, with four Javanese rivers being of particular concern.

tuv.seg: The combined input of the Xi, Dong and Zhujiang Rivers in China all flowing into the South China Sea at the Pearl River delta, was estimated at 0.106 (range 0.091–0.169) million tonnes per year, placing the greater catchment into third position.

L'apport combiné des fleuves Xi, Dong et Zhujiang en Chine, qui se jettent tous dans la mer de Chine méridionale au niveau du delta de la rivière des Perles, a été estimé à 0,106 million de tonnes par an (dans une fourchette de 0,091 à 0,169 million), ce qui place le grand bassin versant en troisième position.

tu.prop: The combined input of the Xi, Dong and Zhujiang Rivers in China all flowing into the South China Sea at the Pearl River delta, was estimated at 0.106 (range 0.091–0.169) million tonnes per year, placing the greater catchment into third position.

The Brantas, Solo, Serayu and Progo Rivers respectively emitting an estimated 38,900 (range 32,300–63,700), 32,500 (range 26,500–54,100), 17,100 (range 13,300–29,900) and 12,800 (range 9,800–22,900) tonnes of plastics per year.

tuv.seg: Indonesia was also identified as a major contributor on the Asian continent, with four Javanese rivers being of particular concern.
L'Indonésie a également été identifiée comme un contributeur majeur sur le continent asiatique, avec quatre rivières javanaises particulièrement préoccupantes.

tu.prop: Indonesia was also identified as a major contributor on the Asian continent, with four Javanese rivers being of particular concern.
Overall, we computed a midpoint annual emission of 200,000 tonnes (14.2% of global total) from Indonesian rivers and streams, mainly coming from the Islands of Java and Sumatra.

tuv.seg: The Brantas, Solo, Serayu and Progo Rivers respectively emitting an estimated 38,900 (range 32,300–63,700), 32,500 (range 26,500–54,100), 17,100 (range 13,300–29,900) and 12,800 (range 9,800–22,900) tonnes of plastics per year.

Les fleuves Brantas, Solo, Serayu et Progo émettent respectivement 38 900 (dans une fourchette de 32 300 à 63 700), 32 500 (dans une fourchette de 26 500 à 54 100), 17 100 (dans une fourchette de 13 300 à 29 900) et 12 800 (dans une fourchette de 9 800 à 22 900) tonnes de plastiques par an.

tu.prop: The Brantas, Solo, Serayu and Progo Rivers respectively emitting an estimated 38,900 (range 32,300–63,700), 32,500 (range 26,500–54,100), 17,100 (range 13,300–29,900) and 12,800 (range 9,800–22,900) tonnes of plastics per year.

This result reflects the levels of population density, as well as waste mismanagement in the region considering the surface area of these catchments is two to three orders of magnitude smaller than other large contributing rivers in the list.

tuv.seg: Overall, we computed a midpoint annual emission of 200,000 tonnes (14.2% of global total) from Indonesian rivers and streams, mainly coming from the Islands of Java and Sumatra.
Dans l'ensemble, nous avons calculé une émission annuelle médiane de 200 000 tonnes (14,2 % du total mondial) provenant des cours d'eau indonésiens, principalement des îles de Java et de Sumatra.

tu.prop: Overall, we computed a midpoint annual emission of 200,000 tonnes (14.2% of global total) from Indonesian rivers and streams, mainly coming from the Islands of Java and Sumatra.

The rest of the world shared the remaining 14% of river plastic mass input, with 7.8% coming from Africa with 109,200 (range 85,700–192,000) tonnes per year, 4.8% from South America with 67,400 (range: 52,700–119,000) tonnes per year, 0.95% from Central and North America with 13,400 (range: 8,880–28,200) tonnes per year, 0.28% from Europe with 3,900 (range: 2,310–9,320) tonnes per year, and the remaining 0.02% from the Australia-Pacific region with 300 (range: 193–707) tonnes per year.

tuv.seg: This result reflects the levels of population density, as well as waste mismanagement in the region considering the surface area of these catchments is two to three orders of magnitude smaller than other large contributing rivers in the list.

Ce résultat reflète les niveaux de densité de population, ainsi que la mauvaise gestion des déchets dans la région, étant donné que la surface de ces bassins versants est de deux à trois fois inférieure à celle d'autres grands cours d'eau étudiés.

tu.prop: This result reflects the levels of population density, as well as waste mismanagement in the region considering the surface area of these catchments is two to three orders of magnitude smaller than other large contributing rivers in the list.

In West Africa particularly, the Cross River with 40,300 (range 33,800–65,100) tonnes per year, the Imo River with 21,500 (range: 17,500–35,100) tonnes per year and the Kwa Ibo River with 11,900 (range: 9,300–20,800) tonnes per year all appeared in the list of the twenty most polluting river catchments.

tuv.seg: The rest of the world shared the remaining 14% of river plastic mass input, with 7.8% coming from Africa with 109,200 (range 85,700–192,000) tonnes per year, 4.8% from South America with 67,400 (range: 52,700–119,000) tonnes per year, 0.95% from Central and North America with 13,400 (range: 8,880–28,200) tonnes per year, 0.28% from Europe with 3,900 (range: 2,310–9,320) tonnes per year, and the remaining 0.02% from the Australia-Pacific region with 300 (range: 193–707) tonnes per year.

Le reste du monde se partageait les 14 % restants de l'apport massique de plastique des rivières, dont 7,8 % provenaient d'Afrique avec 109 200 (fourchette : 85 700-192 000) tonnes par an, 4,8 % d'Amérique du Sud avec 67 400 (fourchette : 52 700-119 000) tonnes par an, 0,95 % d'Amérique centrale et d'Amérique du Nord avec 13 400 (fourchette : 8 880–28 200) tonnes par an, 0,28 % d'Europe avec 3 900 (fourchette : 2 310–9 320) tonnes par an et les 0,02 % restants de la région Australie-Pacifique. avec 300 (plage : 193-707) tonnes par an.

tu.prop: The rest of the world shared the remaining 14% of river plastic mass input, with 7.8% coming from Africa with 109,200 (range 85,700–192,000) tonnes per year, 4.8% from South America with 67,400 (range: 52,700–119,000) tonnes per year, 0.95% from Central and North America with 13,400 (range: 8,880–28,200) tonnes per year, 0.28% from Europe with 3,900 (range: 2,310–9,320) tonnes per year, and the remaining 0.02% from the Australia-Pacific region with 300 (range: 193–707) tonnes per year.

In South America, we estimated an annual input of 38,900 (range 32,200–63,800) tonnes per year coming from the Amazon River, the largest river on earth by water discharge, with tributaries in Peru, Columbia, Ecuador and Brazil.

tuv.seg: In West Africa particularly, the Cross River with 40,300 (range 33,800–65,100) tonnes per year, the Imo River with 21,500 (range: 17,500–35,100) tonnes per year and the Kwa Ibo River with 11,900 (range: 9,300–20,800) tonnes per year all appeared in the list of the twenty most polluting river catchments.

En Afrique de l'Ouest en particulier, la rivière Cross avec 40 300 (dans une fourchette de : 33 800-65 100) tonnes par an, la rivière Imo avec 21 500 (dans une fourchette de : 17 500-35 100) tonnes par an et la rivière Kwa Ibo avec 11 900 (dans une fourchette de : 9 300-20 800) tonnes par an, tous figuraient dans la liste des vingt bassins versants les plus polluants.

tu.prop: In West Africa particularly, the Cross River with 40,300 (range 33,800–65,100) tonnes per year, the Imo River with 21,500 (range: 17,500–35,100) tonnes per year and the Kwa Ibo River with 11,900 (range: 9,300–20,800) tonnes per year all appeared in the list of the twenty most polluting river catchments.
Also in South America, we predicted a significant contribution from the Magdalena River in Columbia with 16,700 (range 12,900–29,500) tonnes per year entering the Gulf of Mexico.

tuv.seg: In South America, we estimated an annual input of 38,900 (range 32,200–63,800) tonnes per year coming from the Amazon River, the largest river on earth by water discharge, with tributaries in Peru, Columbia, Ecuador and Brazil.

En Amérique du Sud, nous avons estimé un apport annuel de 38 900 (dans une fourchette de 32 200 à 63 800) tonnes par an provenant du fleuve Amazone, le plus grand fleuve du monde en termes de débit d'eau, avec des affluents au Pérou, en Colombie, en Équateur et au Brésil.

tu.prop: In South America, we estimated an annual input of 38,900 (range 32,200–63,800) tonnes per year coming from the Amazon River, the largest river on earth by water discharge, with tributaries in Peru, Columbia, Ecuador and Brazil.
Seasonality of river plastic inputs to oceans

tuv.seg: Also in South America, we predicted a significant contribution from the Magdalena River in Columbia with 16,700 (range 12,900–29,500) tonnes per year entering the Gulf of Mexico.

En Amérique du Sud également, nous prévoyons une contribution significative de la rivière Magdalena en Colombie, avec 16 700 (dans une fourchette de 12 900 à 29 500) tonnes par an entrant dans le golfe du Mexique.

tu.prop: Also in South America, we predicted a significant contribution from the Magdalena River in Columbia with 16,700 (range 12,900–29,500) tonnes per year entering the Gulf of Mexico.
Using monthly averaged daily runoff for the period 2005–2014, we assessed seasonal variations in modelled river plastic inputs into oceans worldwide.

tuv.seg: Seasonality of river plastic inputs to oceans
Saisonnalité des apports de plastiques fluviaux dans les océans

tu.prop: Seasonality of river plastic inputs to oceans
We estimated that >74.5% of the total river plastic input occurs between May and October.

tuv.seg: Using monthly averaged daily runoff for the period 2005–2014, we assessed seasonal variations in modelled river plastic inputs into oceans worldwide.

En utilisant le ruissellement quotidien moyen mensuel pour la période de 2005 à 2014, nous avons évalué les variations saisonnières des apports modélisés de plastique des rivières dans les océans du monde entier.

tu.prop: Using monthly averaged daily runoff for the period 2005–2014, we assessed seasonal variations in modelled river plastic inputs into oceans worldwide.
Our model showed a peak in global inputs for the month of August with an estimated 228,800 (range 193,000–375,000) tonnes, and a low for the month of January with 46,200 (range 34,200–87,500) tonnes.

tuv.seg: We estimated that >74.5% of the total river plastic input occurs between May and October.
Nous avons estimé que plus de 74,5 % des émissions totales de plastique dans les rivières se produisent entre mai et octobre.

tu.prop: We estimated that >74.5% of the total river plastic input occurs between May and October.
These seasonal findings were mainly driven by the large inputs from China which are regulated by the East Asian monsoon.

tuv.seg: Our model showed a peak in global inputs for the month of August with an estimated 228,800 (range 193,000–375,000) tonnes, and a low for the month of January with 46,200 (range 34,200–87,500) tonnes.

Notre modèle a montré un pic des apports mondiaux pour le mois d’août avec une estimation de 228 800 (dans une fourchette de 193 000 à 375 000) tonnes, et un minimum pour le mois de janvier avec 46 200 (dans une fourchette de 34 200 à 87 500) tonnes.

tu.prop: Our model showed a peak in global inputs for the month of August with an estimated 228,800 (range 193,000–375,000) tonnes, and a low for the month of January with 46,200 (range 34,200–87,500) tonnes.

In the Asian region, opposing seasons between northern and southern hemispheres create distinct monsoon regimes separated by the South China Sea26, with the Indian and East Asian summer monsoons (June to September) in the North and the South East Asia Summer monsoon (November to March) in the south27 (Fig. 3a).

tuv.seg: These seasonal findings were mainly driven by the large inputs from China which are regulated by the East Asian monsoon.
Ces résultats saisonniers s’expliquent principalement par les apports importants en provenance de Chine, régulés par la mousson d’Asie de l’Est.

tu.prop: These seasonal findings were mainly driven by the large inputs from China which are regulated by the East Asian monsoon.
Figure 3: Seasonality of regional inputs of river plastic to oceans.

tuv.seg: In the Asian region, opposing seasons between northern and southern hemispheres create distinct monsoon regimes separated by the South China Sea26, with the Indian and East Asian summer monsoons (June to September) in the North and the South East Asia Summer monsoon (November to March) in the south27 (Fig. 3a).

Dans la région asiatique, les saisons opposées entre les hémisphères nord et sud créent des régimes de moussons distincts séparés par la mer de Chine méridionale, avec les moussons d'été en Inde et en Asie de l'Est (de juin à septembre) et en Asie du Sud-Est (de novembre à mars) (Données 3a).

tu.prop: In the Asian region, opposing seasons between northern and southern hemispheres create distinct monsoon regimes separated by the South China Sea26, with the Indian and East Asian summer monsoons (June to September) in the North and the South East Asia Summer monsoon (November to March) in the south27 (Fig. 3a).

tuv.seg: Figure 3: Seasonality of regional inputs of river plastic to oceans.
Figure 3 : Saisonnalité Des Apports Régionaux De Plastique Fluvial Dans Les Océans.
